# Implementation Plan: Dashboard Real-Time Command Updates

**Branch**: `006-dashboard-websocket-updates` | **Date**: 2026-05-15 | **Spec**: [spec.md](./spec.md)  
**Input**: Feature specification from `/specs/006-dashboard-websocket-updates/spec.md`

**Note**: This plan was generated by `/speckit-plan`. It extends the existing local dashboard with WebSocket-delivered live updates for dashboard-visible command activity while preserving normal CLI behavior when the dashboard service is not running.

## Summary

Add a real-time update layer to the existing local dashboard so connected browser views update when CLI commands or dashboard-triggered workflows start, progress, complete, fail, or change dashboard-visible state. The dashboard service owns the browser WebSocket endpoint, keeps an in-memory live event bus, publishes redacted command activity and state-invalidation events, and lets browser pages re-read existing dashboard APIs for authoritative current state.

External CLI commands discover the running dashboard through an ephemeral local runtime session file. If the service is available, commands send non-blocking localhost update events with a short timeout; if the service is absent, stale, unreachable, or stopped, commands continue silently with the same primary output and audit behavior they have today. Dashboard startup and reconnect flows load current persisted state from existing config, auth, audit, research, and table services rather than claiming missed live events were delivered.

## Technical Context

**Language/Version**: TypeScript 5.9.x on Node.js `>=22`, matching `package.json`.  
**Primary Dependencies**: Existing `@oclif/core`, `conf`, `open`, `zod`, and Node standard libraries remain in use. Add a focused WebSocket server/client dependency for the dashboard live channel because the current local HTTP server is built on `node:http` and the repo has no WebSocket server implementation.  
**Storage**: Existing config/auth/audit/research files remain the source of truth. Add only an ephemeral dashboard runtime session file under the existing local app data area for service discovery. No persistent dashboard database or durable live-event queue is introduced. Browser storage remains limited to dashboard UI preferences and session UI state.  
**Testing**: Vitest unit and integration tests. Integration tests start an in-process dashboard server, connect WebSocket clients, run dashboard-triggered workflows and CLI command hooks, and verify live events, reconnect behavior, redaction, ordering, and dashboard-absent no-op behavior. Browser-visible evidence remains covered by the dashboard validation workflow from `specs/005-dashboard-e2e-validation/`.  
**Target Platform**: Developer workstations and AI-agent execution environments that can run the Node CLI and open or visit the localhost dashboard.  
**Project Type**: Single TypeScript CLI package with a local web-service dashboard surface.  
**Performance Goals**: Connected browser views show 95% of command start events within 1 second and 95% of command outcome events within 2 seconds during local validation. Reconnected views catch up to current state within 3 seconds. Dashboard-absent CLI runs add no user-visible warnings or prompts.  
**Constraints**: Local-only by default. No dashboard/web login. Lark auth remains required for Lark-backed data. Live update delivery must never fail the primary command result. Live update payloads must be redacted, must preserve source-controlled values, and must not overwrite unsaved dashboard form drafts. Manual refresh remains available and must converge to the same final state.  
**Scale/Scope**: One local dashboard service process, multiple connected browser tabs, supported CLI commands and dashboard-triggered workflows in the same local environment, seven dashboard pages, existing API surfaces, and focused new live contracts. This feature does not add a hosted multi-user service, remote dashboard sharing, or a durable message broker.

## Constitution Check

_GATE: Must pass before Phase 0 research. Re-check after Phase 1 design._

- **AI consumer contract**: PASS. The plan defines explicit live event envelopes, state invalidation scopes, delivery outcomes, runtime discovery state, client connection states, and validation evidence paths so AI agents can reason about the feature without guessing.
- **Evidence sources**: PASS. Planning evidence comes from [spec.md](./spec.md), [package.json](../../package.json), existing dashboard implementation files under `src/dashboard/`, CLI command/audit flow in [base-command.ts](../../src/cli/base-command.ts), existing dashboard contracts in `specs/004-add-dashboard-command/contracts/`, dashboard validation plan in `specs/005-dashboard-e2e-validation/plan.md`, current tests under `tests/`, and constitution rules in `.specify/memory/constitution.md`.
- **Unsupported-claim prevention**: PASS. Live events are treated as notifications and invalidation hints, not independent proof of final facts. Browser pages must fetch authoritative state from existing APIs, and reports must distinguish live-delivered events from startup, manual refresh, browser refresh, and reconnect catch-up.
- **Reproducible validation**: PASS. The plan defines deterministic Vitest coverage for WebSocket connection, event ordering, redaction, reconnect catch-up, multi-client convergence, dashboard-triggered workflows, external CLI dashboard-absent no-op behavior, and runtime session cleanup. Browser evidence is documented in the quickstart.
- **Report traceability**: PASS. Contracts and quickstart require each pass/fail/blocked live-update conclusion to record the triggering command, affected dashboard surface, observed event, final API state, timing, evidence artifact, and any blocked dependency.

Post-design re-check: PASS. [research.md](./research.md), [data-model.md](./data-model.md), [contracts/dashboard-live-contract.md](./contracts/dashboard-live-contract.md), [contracts/command-event-ingress-contract.md](./contracts/command-event-ingress-contract.md), [contracts/browser-live-ui-contract.md](./contracts/browser-live-ui-contract.md), and [quickstart.md](./quickstart.md) preserve evidence-backed outputs, no unsupported claims, reproducible validation, redaction, local-only scope, dashboard-absent no-op behavior, and no persistent dashboard database.

## Project Structure

### Documentation (this feature)

```text
specs/006-dashboard-websocket-updates/
├── plan.md
├── research.md
├── data-model.md
├── quickstart.md
├── contracts/
│   ├── browser-live-ui-contract.md
│   ├── command-event-ingress-contract.md
│   └── dashboard-live-contract.md
├── checklists/
│   └── requirements.md
└── tasks.md
```

`tasks.md` is intentionally not created by `/speckit-plan`; it is the expected output of `/speckit-tasks`.

### Source Code (repository root)

```text
package.json
AGENTS.md
src/
├── cli/
│   ├── base-command.ts
│   └── commands/
│       └── dashboard.ts
├── dashboard/
│   ├── api.ts
│   ├── assets.ts
│   ├── live-client.ts
│   ├── live-events.ts
│   ├── live-runtime.ts
│   ├── live-server.ts
│   ├── routes.ts
│   ├── schemas.ts
│   └── server.ts
├── audit/
├── config/
└── reporting/

tests/
├── fixtures/
│   └── dashboard.ts
├── integration/
│   ├── dashboard-command.test.ts
│   ├── dashboard-http.test.ts
│   └── dashboard-live.test.ts
└── unit/
    ├── dashboard-assets.test.ts
    ├── dashboard-live-events.test.ts
    ├── dashboard-live-runtime.test.ts
    ├── dashboard-redaction.test.ts
    └── dashboard-schemas.test.ts
```

**Structure Decision**: Keep the existing single-package CLI architecture. Add focused live-update modules under `src/dashboard/` because the service, browser assets, route contracts, redaction, and dashboard state surfaces already live there. Touch `src/cli/base-command.ts` only to emit lifecycle events through a best-effort live client after normal command output/audit paths remain intact. Keep config, auth, audit, and research persistence in their existing modules.

## Complexity Tracking

No Constitution Check violations require justification. The added complexity is limited to a local WebSocket channel, ephemeral runtime discovery, and focused contracts around existing dashboard surfaces; it avoids a hosted service, durable queue, database, or separate frontend package.
